subject: Undeploy k8s artifacts on timeout
commit_message:
Undeploy k8s artifacts on timeout

Issue-ID: DCAEGEN2-987
Change-Id: I35a2dcdb7b7f1b7280dba6593a9ee2b8921b6df9
Signed-off-by: Jack Lucas <jflucas@research.att.com>

entries.message:
Patch Set 1: Verified+1

Build Successful 

https://jenkins.onap.org/job/dcaegen2-platform-plugins-master-verify-java/135/ : SUCCESS

Logs: https://logs.onap.org/production/vex-yul-ecomp-jenkins-1/dcaegen2-platform-plugins-master-verify-java/135

Patch Set 1: Code-Review+1

looks good to me.

Patch Set 1:

To me, the fact that the container eventually *does* come up tells me that this isn't a non-recoverable error. Non-recoverable to me is for scenarios where "it will never work in the current state" rather than  "it might work sometime in the future ". Why not make this a recoverable error and retry (I think we'd need a max_retries). 

Re whether to do automatic cleanups, this is consistent with how the CDAP broker used to function. If anything failed during an install, it would attempt to clean up "as much as it possibly could"

Patch Set 1:

The condition here is that the client has told the plugin to deploy a component and wait a certain amount of time for the component to become "ready" (i.e., to start passing its health check, aka readiness probe). The plugin has successfully made the deployment request to Kubernetes, has probed periodically to see if the component has become ready, and has reached the time limit without the component becoming ready.  In effect, we treat each probe that returns a "not ready" status as a recoverable error--we wait a bit, and try again.  Eventually we give up and throw a NonRecoverableError.

The plugin has a default maximum wait time.  It's possible to override this in the blueprint and even to set it to infinity.  

(Note that Kubernetes sends a success response to a request to deploy a set of entities very quickly, after doing very minimal checks.  It doesn't check for what seem to be very obvious problems, like asking to spin up containers from Docker images that don't exist.  It doesn't even pick a host where it will deploy things before it responds. Kubernetes is relentlessly optimistic.  The image for this container doesn't exist?  Keep trying.  Maybe someone will push it to the repo.  Maybe someone will come along and edit the container description and change the image name to one that will work.  It's a different way of doing things.)

Patch Set 1: Code-Review+2

The patch address the most common issue we are encountering in ONAP deployment. As for proposal to generalize the solution for all nonrecoverable error - need to assess the impact; this can be tracked as separate jira.
Change has been successfully merged by Vijay Venkatesh Kumar
